summary:
Trauma Systems & Kinematics

Trauma System Overview

A trauma system is an organized, coordinated effort in a defined geographic area to deliver the full spectrum of care to injured patients — from prevention and prehospital care through definitive rehabilitation. The trauma system is anchored by trauma centers, which are hospitals verified by the American College of Surgeons (ACS) and designated by state or local authorities to provide specific levels of trauma care.

Trauma Center Levels

LevelResources & CapabilitiesKey Requirements
Level IComprehensive regional resource — the highest level; provides total care from prevention through rehabilitation24/7 in-house general surgery, ortho, neuro, anesthesia; OR teams, ICU, research & teaching; ≥1,200 trauma patients/year or ≥240 ISS >15
Level IIDefinitive trauma care similar to Level I, without research/teaching requirements24/7 availability of specialists (may be on-call); definitive care for 90%+ of trauma patients
Level IIIAssessment, resuscitation, emergency surgery, stabilization & transfer to Level I/II24/7 ED coverage; general surgery available; transfer agreements with higher-level centers
Level IVATLS stabilization and transfer; typically in rural/remote areas24/7 emergency provider coverage; ATLS-trained; transfer protocols
NREMT Exam Pearl: You will NOT be asked to distinguish Level I vs Level II in detail. However, you MUST know the trauma center criteria — the "when to transport to a trauma center" decision based on physiology, anatomy, mechanism of injury, and special considerations. This is high-yield for the NREMT cognitive exam.

Trauma Center Transport Decision Criteria

The decision to transport to a trauma center is based on four categories. Meeting ANY ONE criterion in the physiologic or anatomic category warrants transport to the highest-level trauma center within the system.

1. Physiologic Criteria
  • GCS ≤13
  • SBP <90 mmHg
  • RR <10 or >29 (<20 in infants <1 year)
  • Need for ventilatory support
2. Anatomic Criteria
  • Penetrating injury to head, neck, torso, or extremities proximal to elbow/knee
  • Flail chest
  • Two or more proximal long-bone fractures
  • Crushed, degloved, or mangled extremity
  • Amputation proximal to wrist or ankle
  • Pelvic fracture
  • Open or depressed skull fracture
  • Paralysis (suspected spinal cord injury)
3. Mechanism of Injury (MOI) Criteria
  • Falls: Adult >20 ft (≈2 stories); Child >10 ft or 2-3× child's height
  • High-risk auto crash: Intrusion >12 in occupant compartment or >18 in any site; Ejection; Death in same compartment
  • Auto vs pedestrian/bicyclist: Thrown, run over, or >20 mph impact
  • Motorcycle crash >20 mph or separation from bike
4. Special Considerations
  • Age: Adults >55 with hypotension or AMS; children — consider pediatric trauma center
  • Anticoagulant/antiplatelet use
  • Pregnancy >20 weeks
  • Burns with trauma
  • Provider judgment
Key Concept: Any ONE criterion in physiologic OR anatomic = transport to highest-level trauma center. MOI and special considerations may trigger transport based on local protocols.

Kinematics of Trauma

Kinematics describes how energy is transferred to the body during injury. The core principle: if the mechanism fits, assume the injury until proven otherwise.

The Three Collisions of an MVC

  1. Vehicle hits object — crumple zones absorb energy; intrusion >12 in = high-energy event
  2. Body hits interior — steering wheel, dashboard, windshield, seat belt cause blunt trauma
  3. Organs hit body structures — brain hits skull (TBI, subdural), heart shears at great vessels (aortic transection), liver/spleen tear at attachments (intra-abdominal hemorrhage)

Laws of Physics in Trauma

KE = ½mv² — velocity dominates. Doubling speed quadruples energy. Rapid deceleration (<0.1 s) = massive force. Airbags and crumple zones extend deceleration time.

Blunt vs Penetrating Trauma

FeatureBluntPenetrating
MechanismForce over broad area (MVC, fall)Force concentrated at entry (GSW, stab)
External findingsSubtle — "tip of the iceberg"Obvious wound(s), extent hidden
Common injuriesSolid organ laceration, pelvic fx, TBIVascular injury, hollow viscus perf, hemo/pneumothorax
EMS provider approachHigh suspicion based on MOI; assume hidden injuriesWound assessment (entrance AND exit); control hemorrhage; do not remove impaled objects

Predicting Injury Patterns by MOI

Frontal MVC: Up-and-over (head→chest→abdomen) vs down-and-under (knees→lumbar→viscus). Restrained: seat belt syndrome (clavicle/rib fx, hollow viscus injury).
Lateral MVC: Less crumple zone → more energy. Spleen (left) / liver (right) compressed against spine. Pelvic fracture common.
Rear MVC: Whiplash — acceleration-deceleration cervical spine injury.
Rollover: Ejection = death or severe injury. Multiple force vectors.
Motorcycle: No protective shell. Waddell's triad: femur/tibia fx + facial/head + upper extremity injury.
Pedestrian vs auto: Adult: leg bumper → hood (pelvis, chest, head). Child: pelvis/thigh → thrown forward. Waddell's triad: head + chest/abdomen + femur.

Wound Ballistics

Low-energy (knife): injury along blade track. Medium-energy (handgun): permanent + temporary cavity. High-energy (rifle): massive cavitation, fragment wounds. Shotgun: close range = single devastating projectile; longer range = dispersed pellets.

Blast Injury Patterns

Primary: Over-pressurization wave — tympanic membrane rupture, lung injury, bowel perforation.
Secondary: Fragments — penetrating trauma.
Tertiary: Victim thrown — blunt trauma, fractures.
Quaternary: Burns, crush, inhalation.
Quinary: Delayed effects from contamination.

Critical Warning: Penetrating torso trauma with instability or peritonitis = immediate transport. "Scoop and run" — not "stay and play."

quiz.q: A 28-year-old male was in a high-speed T-bone MVC on the driver's side. He complains of severe LUQ pain and left hip pain. Based on MOI, which organ is at HIGHEST risk?
quiz.explanation: In a left-sided lateral impact, the spleen is compressed against the spine — the most vulnerable left-sided solid organ.

quiz.q: Which physiologic criterion alone warrants transport to the highest-level trauma center?
quiz.explanation: SBP <90 mmHg is an independent physiologic criterion. GCS ≤13 (not 14), RR <10 or >29 (28 is under the threshold).

quiz.q: [HARD] An unrestrained driver struck a tree at 50 mph. The steering wheel is bent and the patient has a contusion across his chest in the shape of the wheel. His vitals are stable. Which injury should you have the HIGHEST suspicion for?
quiz.explanation: Steering wheel deformity + chest wall imprint = high-energy transfer to chest. Suspect cardiac contusion, pulmonary contusion, possible great-vessel injury — regardless of stable vitals.

quiz.q: An adult patient falls from an 25-foot window. He is awake with no visible injuries and stable vitals. What is the appropriate transport decision?
options: No trauma center — fall was not >20 ft
Transport to trauma center — fall >20 ft is a mechanism criterion
Transport — any fall >10 ft warrants trauma evaluation
quiz.explanation: The adult fall criterion is >20 ft. At 18 ft, this is borderline — but clinical judgment should still prompt strong consideration for a trauma center.

flashcards.question: What are the four levels of trauma centers and their basic roles?
flashcards.answer: Level I: Comprehensive regional resource, 24/7 in-house specialists, research & teaching. Level II: Similar care without research, on-call specialists. Level III: Assessment, resuscitation, stabilization & transfer. Level IV: ATLS stabilization in remote areas before transfer.

flashcards.question: List the FOUR categories of trauma center transport criteria.
flashcards.answer: (1) Physiologic — GCS ≤13, SBP <90, RR <10 or >29, need ventilatory support. (2) Anatomic — specific injury patterns. (3) Mechanism — falls >20 ft, high-risk auto crash, pedestrian struck. (4) Special considerations — age >55, anticoagulants, pregnancy >20 weeks, burns + trauma.

flashcards.question: What are the three collisions in an MVC?
flashcards.answer: (1) Vehicle collides with object — crumple zones absorb energy. (2) Body collides with interior — blunt trauma from steering wheel, dashboard, windshield. (3) Organs collide with body structures — brain hits skull, heart shears at great vessels, liver/spleen tear.

flashcards.question: Why does doubling speed quadruple injury severity?
flashcards.answer: KE = ½mv². Velocity is squared, so doubling speed increases crash energy by 4×. A 60 mph crash releases 4× more energy than a 30 mph crash.

flashcards.question: What injury patterns are associated with frontal impact MVC?
flashcards.answer: Up-and-over: Head hits windshield (TBI) → chest hits steering wheel (sternal fx, cardiac contusion) → abdomen (liver/spleen). Down-and-under: Knees hit dash (femur fx) → lap belt loads lumbar spine (Chance fracture) → hollow viscus rupture.

flashcards.question: Distinguish blunt vs penetrating trauma energy transfer.
flashcards.answer: Blunt: Energy dissipates over broad area — widespread but less obvious injury. Penetrating: Energy concentrated at entry point — injury tracks deep; external wound is the 'tip of the iceberg.'

flashcards.question: What are the five blast injury phases?
flashcards.answer: Primary (over-pressurization wave), Secondary (fragments), Tertiary (victim thrown), Quaternary (burns, crush, inhalation), Quinary (delayed contamination effects).

flashcards.question: What is Waddell's triad?
flashcards.answer: Motorcycle vs car (30+ mph): femur/tibia fracture + facial/head injury + upper extremity injury. Pediatric pedestrian: head injury + chest/abdominal injury + femur fracture.

critical.scenario: You are at a single-vehicle MVC. A 34-year-old unrestrained driver struck a concrete barrier at 55 mph. The front end is severely deformed with engine compartment intrusion. The patient has a large ecchymotic steering wheel imprint on his chest. Vitals: BP 106/74, HR 116, RR 26, SpO₂ 93%, GCS 15. Auscultation reveals diminished breath sounds on the left with crackles on the right.
critical.question: Based on the three-collision model and MOI, what injuries must you suspect and what is your transport priority?

options.t: Treat for simple rib contusion — apply oxygen, transport non-emergently; the steering wheel contusion is likely superficial
options.f: Incorrect. A steering-wheel deformity at 55 mph indicates massive energy transfer to the chest. Suspect cardiac contusion, pulmonary contusion, and possible aortic injury.

options.t: Suspect cardiac contusion, bilateral pulmonary contusion, possible left hemothorax/pneumothorax, and possible aortic injury — apply high-flow oxygen, rapid trauma assessment, expedite transport to a Level I trauma center
options.f: Correct. This patient meets both anatomic (chest wall injury) and physiologic (tachypnea, hypoxia) criteria for a trauma center.

critical.explanation: Frontal impact with steering wheel deformity at 55 mph transmits massive force. Cardiac contusion → dysrhythmias. Pulmonary contusion → hypoxia worsening over 6-24 hours. Diminished breath sounds → hemothorax/pneumothorax. Aortic transection is the 'silent killer' in high-speed deceleration.
critical.kpi: High-risk MOI identification and trauma center triage

critical.scenario: A 42-year-old male fell 25 feet from scaffolding onto gravel, landing feet-first then backward. He is awake (GCS 15), complains of severe low back pain, bilateral heel pain, and difficulty moving his legs. Vitals: BP 118/74, HR 104. Tenderness over lumbar spine, swelling in both heels. Sensation intact, motor strength 3/5 bilaterally.
critical.question: What injury pattern do you suspect from this axial loading mechanism?

options.t: Bilateral calcaneal fractures only — immobilize lower extremities, transport to closest hospital; back pain is muscular
options.f: Incorrect. Axial loading from 25 ft transmits force up the skeleton. Lumbar spine fracture is highly probable. Motor weakness is a red flag for spinal cord compromise.

options.t: Axial loading injury: bilateral calcaneal fractures + possible lumbar burst fracture + possible spinal cord injury — full spinal immobilization, rapid trauma assessment, transport to trauma center
options.f: Correct. This meets MOI criterion (fall >20 ft). Motor weakness despite intact sensation suggests spinal cord compression.

critical.explanation: Feet-first landing from >20 ft causes axial loading through calcanei, tibia/fibula, pelvis, into lumbar spine (burst fracture). Motor weakness suggests spinal cord edema/compression. Immobilize entire spine and transport to trauma center.
critical.kpi: Axial loading injury recognition and spinal precautions

critical.scenario: A 7-year-old male was struck by a car at 25 mph. He has a deformed right thigh (mid-shaft), left chest contusion, 3 cm chin laceration, and is holding his abdomen. Vitals: BP 98/62, HR 130, RR 28. Weight ~22 kg.
critical.question: What injury pattern (Waddell's triad) do you suspect in this pediatric pedestrian?

options.t: The femur fracture is the priority — splint and transport to the closest hospital
options.f: Incorrect. Waddell's triad in pediatric pedestrians includes head injury + chest/abdominal injury + femur fracture. Abdominal tenderness could indicate life-threatening internal injuries.

options.t: Suspect Waddell's triad: head injury (chin laceration, possible TBI) + chest/abdominal injury + femur fracture — spinal precautions, rapid assessment, traction splint for femur, transport to a pediatric trauma center
options.f: Correct. Abdominal tenderness and chest contusion raise concern for intra-abdominal and intrathoracic injury. The femur fracture may have caused significant blood loss.

critical.explanation: Waddell's triad in pediatric pedestrians: head injury + chest/abdominal injury (liver/spleen) + femur fracture (500-1000 mL blood loss). The child requires a pediatric trauma center for multi-system evaluation.
